Hmm, 5 ≠ 7 and 14 ≠ 7. So maybe the goal isn't to match sizes.

What if the game is about clicking on the empty cells inside the U-shapes to "transfer" pixels from the bar? Each click transfers some number of pixels. The green bar lost 9 pixels from 1 click, and the yellow bar lost 9 pixels from 1 click.
But wait, the green bar went from 14 to 5, which is a loss of 9. And the yellow bar went from 23 to 14, which is also a loss of 9. So each click removes 9 pixels from the bar.
Actually, let me reconsider. The green bar was 3×5 = 15 cells, minus 1 red dot = 14 green pixels. After clicking, it became 3×2 = 6 cells, minus 1 red dot = 5 green pixels. So it lost 9 green pixels.
The yellow bar was 8×3 = 24 cells, minus 1 red dot = 23 yellow pixels. After clicking, it became 5×3 = 15 cells, minus 1 red dot = 14 yellow pixels. So it lost 9 yellow pixels.
Both lost 9 pixels. The green bar went from height 3, width 5 to height 3, width 2. The yellow bar went from height 8, width 3 to height 5, width 3. So the bars are shrinking along their longer dimension.
Green bar: 3×5 → 3×2 (shrank from 5 to 2 along the width, losing 3 columns × 3 rows = 9 pixels)
Yellow bar: 8×3 → 5×3 (shrank from 8 to 5 along the height, losing 3 rows × 3 columns = 9 pixels)
So each click removes a 3×3 block from the bar. The green bar lost 3 columns (9 pixels), and the yellow bar lost 3 rows (9 pixels).
